payload.formula_text:
Profit = Revenue - Ad Spend
ROAS = Revenue / Ad Spend
Revenue Per Session = Revenue / Sessions

Profitability Tier:
- OWNED/EARNED: No ad spend (Direct, Organic, Email)
- HIGH PROFIT: ROAS >= 4x
- MODERATE PROFIT: ROAS 2-4x
- LOW PROFIT/LOSS: ROAS < 2x
payload.formula_explanation: Comprehensive table showing profitability metrics for each marketing channel. Channels are classified into profitability tiers based on ROAS. Owned and earned channels (Direct, Organic, Email) have no ad spend and are inherently profitable.
how_to_read: Table sorted by revenue descending - highest-revenue channels at top
Revenue shows total attributed revenue from GA4 for the month
Spend shows ad costs mapped from Google Ads and Meta Ads
Profit column shows green for positive, red for negative
ROAS shows return on ad spend - higher is better, colored by threshold
Profitability Tier badge provides quick assessment of channel health
Click any row to navigate to that channel detailed dashboard
decisions_answered: Which channels are most profitable after accounting for ad spend?
What is the ROAS for each paid channel?
Which channels should we scale vs. reduce spending on?
How much are owned channels (Email, Organic) contributing?
payload.narration_text:
This table provides a complete profitability breakdown for every marketing channel.

Each row shows a channel with its revenue, ad spend, profit, ROAS, revenue per session, and profitability tier. Channels are sorted by revenue to show the biggest contributors first.

The Profitability Tier column gives you a quick assessment. OWNED/EARNED channels like Direct, Organic Search, and Email have no ad spend - every dollar of revenue is essentially profit. These are your most valuable channels.

For paid channels, look at ROAS. HIGH PROFIT means 4x or better return on ad spend. MODERATE is 2-4x. LOW PROFIT/LOSS is below 2x - meaning you spend nearly as much as you earn, or more.

Use this to make budget decisions: Scale HIGH PROFIT channels, optimize MODERATE channels, and either fix or reduce LOW PROFIT channels. Click any row to drill into detailed performance.

payload.formula_text:
Retention Rate = Customers purchasing in period / Original cohort size x 100

Cohort = Customers who first purchased in a given month
M0 = Acquisition month (always 100%)
M1 = One month after acquisition, etc.
payload.formula_explanation: Heatmap showing customer retention rates over time for each monthly acquisition cohort. Each row is a cohort of customers who first purchased in that month. Columns show what percentage of that cohort made repeat purchases in subsequent months.
how_to_read: Each row represents a monthly acquisition cohort (e.g., Dec 2025)
Columns show months since acquisition (M0 through M6)
Cell color intensity indicates retention rate - darker = higher retention
M0 is always 100% as this is the acquisition month
M1 is the critical first-repurchase window - target 15-20%+
Compare rows to see if newer cohorts retain better or worse than older cohorts
Click any cell to drill into customer details for that cohort and period
decisions_answered: How well are we retaining customers after acquisition?
What is our typical M1 (first month) retention rate?
Are recent cohorts retaining better or worse than historical cohorts?
When do most customers make their second purchase?
payload.narration_text:
This heatmap visualizes customer retention by monthly acquisition cohort.

Each row represents customers who made their first purchase in that month. The columns show how many of those customers came back in subsequent months - M1 is one month later, M2 is two months, and so on.

M0 is always 100% because that is when they acquired. The critical number is M1 - first month retention. Industry benchmarks for e-commerce suggest 15-20% is healthy. Below 10% indicates significant churn problems.

Color intensity tells the story: Darker cells mean better retention. You want to see darker colors sustained across the row, not rapid fading to light colors.

Compare cohorts vertically: Are recent cohorts showing stronger M1 and M2 retention than older cohorts? This indicates your retention efforts are working. If newer cohorts fade faster, investigate what changed in your acquisition or onboarding.

This answers: Are we building lasting customer relationships, or are customers one and done?

payload.formula_text:
Gross Margin % = (Revenue - COGS - Ad Spend) / Revenue x 100

Estimated COGS = Revenue x 50% (industry benchmark)
Margin After Ads = (Revenue x 50% - Ad Spend) / Revenue
payload.formula_explanation: Tracks gross margin percentage over the past 12 months. Since true COGS data requires ERP integration, this uses an industry benchmark of 50% gross margin on products, then subtracts ad spend to show margin after marketing costs.
how_to_read: X-axis shows months over the trailing 12-month period
Y-axis shows gross margin percentage after ad spend
The green line trends margin over time - look for upward or downward patterns
Each point represents the monthly margin calculation
Subtitle shows current month margin and the 25% target benchmark
Sharp drops may indicate increased ad spend or promotional periods
decisions_answered: Is our gross margin trending up or down over time?
Are we hitting our 25% margin target after ad costs?
How is marketing spend impacting our profitability?
Are there seasonal patterns in our margin performance?
payload.narration_text:
This line chart tracks your gross margin percentage over the past 12 months.

The margin shown is calculated after accounting for estimated product costs and all advertising spend. We use a 50% COGS estimate typical for seed and gardening businesses.

Watch for trend direction - a declining margin may indicate rising ad costs outpacing revenue growth, or increased discounting eroding profitability.

The target benchmark is 25% margin after ads. Months above this line are healthy; consistent performance below suggests you need to either reduce ad spend, improve ad efficiency, or adjust pricing.

Seasonal patterns are common in gardening - expect lower margins during peak ad spending in spring, with higher margins in fall when organic traffic dominates.

payload.formula_text:
CAC = Total Ad Spend / New Paid Customers
LTV = AOV x Purchase Frequency Multiplier (1.5x)

LTV:CAC Ratio = LTV / CAC
Target Ratio: 3:1 (healthy), 2:1 (acceptable)
payload.formula_explanation: Tracks the relationship between Customer Lifetime Value and Customer Acquisition Cost over time. LTV is estimated using a 1.5x multiplier on AOV to account for repeat purchases. CAC is calculated as blended cost across paid channels. The ratio indicates whether customer value justifies acquisition costs.
how_to_read: Green line shows estimated LTV (Lifetime Value) trending over time
Red line shows blended CAC (Customer Acquisition Cost) trending
The gap between lines represents your margin on customer acquisition
Subtitle shows current LTV:CAC ratio and status (HEALTHY/ACCEPTABLE/NEEDS IMPROVEMENT)
LTV should ideally be 3x or more above CAC line
Converging lines indicate deteriorating unit economics
decisions_answered: Are our unit economics healthy (LTV > 3x CAC)?
Is customer acquisition cost rising faster than lifetime value?
Should we invest more in acquisition or focus on retention?
How has customer value vs. acquisition cost changed over time?
payload.narration_text:
This dual-line chart compares Customer Lifetime Value to Customer Acquisition Cost over time.

The green line tracks LTV - the total value you expect from a customer over their relationship with your brand. We estimate this using average order value multiplied by 1.5 to account for repeat purchases.

The red line shows CAC - what you spend on ads to acquire each new customer through paid channels.

The key insight is the gap between these lines. When LTV is 3x or more above CAC, your unit economics are healthy. You can profitably acquire customers. When they converge, you are approaching breakeven or worse.

Watch for trends: Rising CAC with flat LTV signals trouble. Rising LTV with stable CAC indicates improving efficiency. The subtitle shows your current ratio and whether it meets the 3:1 benchmark.

payload.formula_text:
MER = Total Revenue / Total Ad Spend

Total Ad Spend = Google Ads Spend + Meta Ads Spend
payload.formula_explanation: Marketing Efficiency Ratio measures how much revenue is generated for every dollar spent on advertising. A higher MER indicates more efficient marketing - you are generating more revenue per ad dollar. Target MER for e-commerce is typically 3-5x.
how_to_read: Area chart shows MER trending over 12 months
Y-axis shows MER as a multiple (e.g., 4.0x means $4 revenue per $1 ad spend)
Green/blue coloring indicates meeting target (4.0x), orange indicates below target
Subtitle displays current MER value and the 4.0x target
Higher values are better - more revenue per ad dollar
Sharp spikes may indicate reduced ad spend or seasonal revenue increases
decisions_answered: How efficient is our overall marketing spend?
Are we meeting our 4.0x MER target?
Is marketing efficiency improving or declining over time?
Should we increase or decrease ad spend based on efficiency?
payload.narration_text:
This area chart shows your Marketing Efficiency Ratio over the past 12 months.

MER is calculated as total revenue divided by total ad spend across all platforms. A 4.0x MER means you generate $4 in revenue for every $1 spent on ads.

The industry benchmark for e-commerce is 3-5x. Below 3x typically means you are spending too much relative to returns. Above 5x might indicate room to scale - you could potentially spend more while maintaining profitability.

Watch the trend carefully. Declining MER suggests diminishing returns - you may be reaching audience saturation or facing increased competition. Improving MER indicates your marketing optimization is working.

Keep in mind that MER includes all revenue, not just ad-attributed revenue. This makes it a holistic measure of marketing efficiency.

payload.formula_text:
New Customer Revenue = Revenue from first-time purchasers
Returning Customer Revenue = Total Revenue - New Customer Revenue

New % = New Customer Revenue / Total Revenue x 100
payload.formula_explanation: Shows the revenue mix between new customer acquisition and returning customer retention. A healthy mix for mature e-commerce is typically 35-45% new customers. Too high indicates over-reliance on acquisition; too low suggests growth stagnation.
how_to_read: Stacked area chart shows total revenue split by customer type
Blue area represents returning customer revenue (bottom layer)
Orange area represents new customer revenue (top layer)
X-axis shows months over the trailing 12-month period
Subtitle displays current month percentage split
Reference line at 40% indicates industry benchmark for new customer percentage
decisions_answered: What percentage of our revenue comes from new vs returning customers?
Are we over-reliant on new customer acquisition?
Is our retention strategy working to drive repeat purchases?
How is the revenue mix evolving over time?
payload.narration_text:
This stacked area chart shows how your revenue splits between new and returning customers.

The blue area at the bottom represents returning customer revenue - people who have purchased before coming back to buy again. The orange area on top shows new customer revenue - first-time buyers.

A healthy business typically sees 35-45% from new customers and 55-65% from returning customers. If new customer revenue dominates, you may be over-investing in acquisition while neglecting retention. If it is too low, you might be underinvesting in growth.

Watch the trend over time. Healthy businesses often show gradually increasing returning customer percentage as the customer base matures and retention improves. Seasonal spikes in new customer percentage are normal during acquisition-heavy periods.

This directly answers: Are we building a sustainable customer base, or are we on an acquisition treadmill?

payload.formula_text:
Total Revenue = Sum of revenue attributed to each marketing channel
Channel Revenue = GA4-attributed revenue by source/medium grouping
payload.formula_explanation: Shows actual revenue for each marketing channel. Revenue is attributed via GA4 source/medium tracking, grouped into channels like Direct, Organic Search, Paid Search, Email, etc.
how_to_read: Bars represent actual revenue for each channel, sorted by revenue descending
Green bars indicate channels exceeding plan, red bars show channels underperforming
The subtitle shows total variance across all channels and the percentage above/below plan
Longer bars represent higher-revenue channels that have more impact on overall performance
Top channels (Direct, Organic Search) typically drive the majority of revenue
Click any bar to navigate to the detailed channel page for deeper analysis
decisions_answered: Which channels are driving revenue growth vs. underperforming?
Did we hit our overall revenue plan this month?
Where should we investigate performance issues?
Which channels should we consider scaling based on performance?
payload.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows revenue performance against plan for each marketing channel.

Each bar represents a channel's actual revenue for the month, with colors indicating whether performance exceeded or fell short of plan. Green means above plan, red means below.

The channels are sorted by revenue, with your highest-revenue channels at the top. Direct and Organic Search typically dominate, often representing 60-70% of total revenue for established businesses.

The subtitle displays your total variance - the sum of all channel variances - and the overall percentage against plan. A negative variance means you fell short of your 5% growth target.

Focus on channels with the largest absolute variances first. A 5% miss on a $500K channel matters more than a 20% miss on a $20K channel. Click any bar to drill into that channel's detailed performance page.
